s: You are technically always time-traveling — because of Earth's gravity, your feet age slightly slower than your head due to general relativity. Over a 79-year lifetime, your feet are about 90 nanoseconds younger than your head.
q: Due to general relativity, which part of your body ages slightly slower?
a: Your feet (they're in a slightly stronger gravitational field)
d: Your torso (it's in the center of gravity)
Your entire body ages at the same rate
Your hands (they're farther from Earth's center)
Your heart (it's protected by the ribcage)
e: General relativity predicts that time passes slower in stronger gravitational fields. Your feet are closer to Earth's center, experiencing slightly stronger gravity. This has been confirmed with atomic clocks placed at different heights. While 90 nanoseconds is imperceptible, the principle is the same one that requires GPS satellites to correct for relativistic time dilation.

s: China spans five geographical time zones but uses only one official time zone (UTC+8, Beijing Time) for the entire country, meaning sunrise in western China can be as late as 10 AM.
q: How many official time zones does China use despite spanning five geographical ones?
d: Three (Beijing, Shanghai, and Hong Kong Time)
e: Mao Zedong established the single time zone in 1949 to promote national unity. This causes practical absurdities — in Xinjiang, the western province, the sun doesn't set until after 10 PM in summer. Many locals unofficially use 'Xinjiang Time' (UTC+6), two hours behind Beijing, in daily life.

s: There are more trees on Earth than stars in the Milky Way — Earth has roughly 3 trillion trees, while the Milky Way contains an estimated 100 to 400 billion stars.
q: Are there more trees on Earth or stars in the Milky Way?
a: More trees on Earth (about 3 trillion vs. 100-400 billion stars)
d: More stars in the Milky Way (estimated at 5 trillion)
More stars in the Milky Way (estimated at 2 trillion)
They are roughly equal (about 1 trillion each)
Trees and stars are too different to compare
e: A 2015 Yale study using satellite imagery, forest inventories, and supercomputer modeling estimated Earth has 3.04 trillion trees — seven times more than previous estimates. That's about 420 trees per person. However, we're losing about 10 billion trees per year to deforestation.

s: The trolley problem, a famous ethical thought experiment, was first proposed by philosopher Philippa Foot in 1967.
q: Who first proposed the trolley problem thought experiment in 1967?
e: Foot introduced the dilemma of whether to divert a runaway trolley to kill one person instead of five. It became a cornerstone of moral philosophy, illustrating the tension between utilitarian and deontological ethics.

s: During the Dutch Tulip Mania of 1637, a single tulip bulb could sell for more than ten times the annual income of a skilled craftsman.
q: During Dutch Tulip Mania in 1637, a single tulip bulb could cost more than how many times a craftsman's annual income?
e: Tulip Mania is often cited as one of history's first speculative bubbles. At its peak, rare tulip bulbs traded for 10,000 guilders — enough to buy a grand canal house in Amsterdam. The market crashed abruptly in February 1637.

s: Alan Turing proposed the 'imitation game' (now called the Turing Test) in 1950 as a way to evaluate whether a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from a human.
q: In what year did Alan Turing propose the test for machine intelligence?
e: In his paper 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence,' Turing suggested that if a human judge cannot reliably distinguish a machine from a human through text conversation, the machine could be said to 'think.' No machine has definitively passed the test.

s: The longest English word you can type using only the top row of a standard QWERTY keyboard is 'typewriter' — and this is likely not a coincidence, as early typewriter salesmen needed a demo word.
q: What is the longest common English word you can type using only the top row of a QWERTY keyboard?
e: The top row of a QWERTY keyboard contains Q-W-E-R-T-Y-U-I-O-P. 'Typewriter' uses only T-Y-P-E-W-R-I-T-E-R, all from that row. While there's no definitive proof it was intentional, typewriter historians note that early salesmen needed to quickly demonstrate the machines and having the product's own name easily accessible from one row was convenient.

s: The first commercially successful typewriter was the Remington No. 1 (1873), which typed only in uppercase — you couldn't see what you were typing until you pulled the paper out.
q: What was unusual about the Remington No. 1 typewriter (1873)?
a: It typed only uppercase and you couldn't see the text while typing
d: It had no shift key for lowercase letters
It had a very loud mechanical sound
It required hand-cranking to advance the paper
It could only type numbers and symbols
It had no ribbon for the ink
e: The Sholes and Glidden Type-Writer, manufactured by Remington, printed on the underside of the paper. Users had to lift the carriage to check their work. The shift key for lowercase wasn't added until the Remington No. 2 in 1878.

s: Usain Bolt's 100-meter world record of 9.58 seconds, set in 2009, means he averaged about 37.58 km/h (23.35 mph) across the race.
q: What is Usain Bolt's 100-meter world record time, set in 2009?
e: Bolt set this record at the 2009 World Championships in Berlin. His peak speed during the race was even higher — estimated at 44.72 km/h (27.8 mph) between the 60m and 80m marks.

s: Velcro was invented by Swiss engineer George de Mestral in 1941 after he noticed burrs sticking to his dog's fur during a hunting trip.
q: What inspired George de Mestral to invent Velcro?
a: Burrs sticking to his dog's fur
d: A spider's web sticking to clothing
Static electricity from his wool socks
Pine tree needles clinging to fabric
A child's sticky fingers after eating candy
e: De Mestral examined the burrs under a microscope and discovered tiny hooks on their surface that caught on the loops of fabric and fur. He spent eight years perfecting a synthetic version of this hook-and-loop system, patenting it in 1955.

s: Vending machines kill more people per year than sharks do — on average, about 2-3 people die annually from vending machine tip-overs, while shark attacks kill about 5-10 people worldwide, but the ratio per encounter is dramatically different.
q: On average, which causes more human deaths per year?
a: They're roughly comparable — both kill only a handful of people per year
d: Vending machines by far (killing over 100 people per year)
Shark attacks by far (killing over 50 people per year)
Falling vending machines (killing over 10 people yearly)
Shark attacks in developed countries only
They kill equal numbers of people on average
e: The comparison is misleading in context: billions of people interact with vending machines daily, making them statistically very safe per encounter. Only a few hundred million people enter the ocean each year. The real takeaway is that shark attacks are extraordinarily rare — you're more likely to be killed by a falling coconut, a cow, or a lightning strike.

s: A day on Venus is longer than its year — Venus takes 243 Earth days to rotate once on its axis, but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.
q: On which planet does a single day last longer than its entire year?
e: Venus also rotates backward compared to most planets (retrograde rotation), so the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east. One theory is that a massive collision billions of years ago flipped Venus upside down. Because of this odd rotation, a solar day on Venus (sunrise to sunrise) is actually about 117 Earth days.

s: The wheel was invented around 3500 BCE in Mesopotamia, but it was first used for pottery — not transportation.
q: What was the wheel first used for when it was invented around 3500 BCE?
e: The potter's wheel appeared centuries before wheels were attached to carts. The oldest known wheel used for transport dates to around 3200 BCE, found in present-day Slovenia.
